skill.name: exploratory-data-analysis
skill.description: Analyze scientific data files across common chemistry, genomics, imaging, spectroscopy, omics, and general data formats. The skill detects file types, extracts basic metadata, runs format-aware checks where supported, and creates markdown EDA reports.
skill.summary: Generate exploratory data analysis reports for scientific data files.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, weak crypto, and sensitive-file patterns, but review found these are mostly false positives from markdown code spans, scientific acronyms, and SAM file format documentation. The confirmed risk is expected local filesystem processing: the analyzer reads user-supplied files, includes absolute paths in generated reports, and writes markdown output. No prompt injection attempt, malicious exfiltration, or hidden network behavior was found.

medium_findings.title: Local Filesystem Analysis and Report Writes
medium_findings.description: The analyzer accepts a user-provided path, reads local data files, and writes a markdown report. This is core functionality, but it can expose private data if run on sensitive files or if reports are shared without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly stats and reads the requested file path and writes report output. The behavior is legitimate for EDA, but the filesystem access is real.

medium_findings.title: Generated Reports Include Absolute Local Paths
medium_findings.description: The script records the absolute input file path and prints it into the generated report. This can disclose usernames, project names, or directory layout when reports are shared.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The absolute path is stored in basic_info and later appended to the report. The privacy impact depends on how users share generated reports.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer treated markdown backticks and fenced examples as shell execution. Reviewed locations are documentation or report-template text, not Ruby backtick execution or subprocess calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown examples and inline code spans. No executable shell invocation is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Sensitive and Weak-Crypto Findings Are Scientific Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: Flags for Windows SAM, certificate keys, and weak cryptography map to scientific file names, dictionary keys, or documentation terms. No evidence found of credential harvesting, cryptographic implementation, or access to Windows security databases.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe SAM sequence alignment files and ordinary dictionary or HDF5 keys. They do not reference secrets or cryptographic routines.

low_findings.title: Archive Library Mentions Are Reference Documentation
low_findings.description: The reference files mention Python archive libraries for ZIP and TAR formats. The executable analyzer does not implement archive extraction in the reviewed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are format documentation and library names. No extraction loop or archive write path is present in the analyzer evidence reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Local File Path Processing
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI takes a user-supplied file path and reads the target file for analysis. This requires user trust in the selected input path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code reads JSON and HDF5 content from the provided path. This is expected behavior, but it is still a real local file access pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Report File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The script writes generated markdown output to a provided or derived output path. Users should avoid overwriting important files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write call is direct and confirmed. The risk is limited because the output path is visible CLI behavior.

content.value_statement: Scientific data files can be hard to inspect before analysis. This skill detects formats, summarizes structure and quality, and produces a markdown report with next steps.
actual_capabilities: Detects scientific file categories from extensions and reference documentation.
Analyzes CSV, TSV, JSON, NumPy, HDF5, FASTA, FASTQ, TIFF, and common image files when libraries are installed.
Extracts file size, modified time, extension, format category, and selected structural metadata.
Samples large tabular and sequencing files to produce practical summaries.
Generates markdown reports with findings, quality notes, and downstream analysis recommendations.
Provides reference guidance for chemistry, genomics, imaging, spectroscopy, proteomics, metabolomics, and general formats.
limitations: Many specialized formats require optional Python libraries that may not be installed.
The bundled analyzer supports a subset of the documented 200+ formats directly.
Large files are sampled, so results may not represent every record.
The skill does not validate scientific conclusions or replace domain expert review.

use_cases.title: Screen new lab datasets
use_cases.description: Create a quick report on file structure, quality signals, and recommended preprocessing before deeper analysis.

use_cases.title: Inspect sequencing files
use_cases.description: Summarize FASTA or FASTQ content, sequence lengths, quality values, and next analysis steps.

use_cases.title: Document shared data packages
use_cases.description: Generate readable markdown summaries that explain file formats, metadata, and practical limitations for collaborators.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze one file
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this scientific data file and create an exploratory data analysis summary: [file path].

prompt_templates.title: Check data quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect [file path] for structure, missing values, quality issues, and format-specific risks. Save a markdown report.

prompt_templates.title: Compare related files
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these related scientific files: [file paths]. Compare formats, dimensions, metadata, and quality signals.

prompt_templates.title: Plan downstream analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Run EDA on [file path], identify analysis-ready fields, flag limitations, and recommend domain-specific preprocessing and visualization steps.

output_examples.input: A FASTQ file from a sequencing run.
output: A markdown report identifies the file as FASTQ data.
It summarizes sampled read counts, read length range, mean quality, and recommended quality control steps.
It notes required libraries when deeper parsing is unavailable.

output_examples.input: A CSV file with experimental measurements.
output: A report lists columns, data types, missing value counts, and numeric summaries.
It recommends correlation checks, outlier review, and normalization choices based on the observed data.
A report describes image size, mode, array shape, value range, and intensity summary.
It suggests image quality checks, background correction, segmentation, and intensity measurement workflows.

best_practices: Use copies of sensitive datasets when generating reports that may be shared.
Install only the scientific libraries needed for the file formats you plan to inspect.
Review sampled results against full-dataset checks before making final decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not treat sampled summaries as complete validation for very large datasets.
Do not publish generated reports without removing sensitive paths or metadata.
Do not force unsupported proprietary formats through generic readers without confirming the parser.

faq.question: What file types can this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It documents 200+ scientific formats and directly analyzes common tabular, array, sequence, HDF5, JSON, and image files.

faq.question: Does it install scientific libraries automatically?
faq.answer: No. It reports missing libraries and suggests packages, but installation remains under user control.

faq.question: Can it handle very large files?
faq.answer: Yes, for some formats it uses sampling or chunk-friendly approaches. Full validation may require separate domain tools.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network upload or external service calls in the reviewed files.

faq.question: What output does it create?
faq.answer: It creates a markdown exploratory data analysis report with file details, summaries, quality notes, and recommendations.

faq.question: Is it safe for private research data?
faq.answer: Use caution. Generated reports can include absolute local paths and metadata, so review reports before sharing.
